snapshot.stmt: For every P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon, under ass:consistency, ass:iv-exogeneity, ass:threshold-selection, ass:uniform-resistance, ass:rank-shape-y0, ass:rank-shape-y1, and ass:rank-association-floor, Q\in\mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P) implies r^Q\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P) and \delta_Q=\Delta(r^Q). Conversely, each r\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P) equals r^Q for a Q\in\mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P).

defs.def:response-polytope: \mathcal R_\infty(P)=\{r\in\mathbb R_+^{M\times K\times J\times J}:\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}=\lambda_k\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M,\ k=1,\ldots,K;\ A_m^Pr=(b_{d\ell hm})_{d\in\{0,1\},\ell=1,\ldots,L,h=1,\ldots,J}\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J\}.
defs.def:feasible-observed-law-domain: \mathcal P_\epsilon=\{P\in\mathcal P(\mathcal W):\alpha_m>0\ \forall m;\ \pi_{\ell m}>0\ \forall\ell,m;\ \{p_{m\ell}:\ell=1,\ldots,L\}=\{q_k:k=0,\ldots,K\}\ \forall m;\ \mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\ne\varnothing\}.
defs.def:compatible-laws: \mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P)=\{Q:Q_W=P;\ Y=DY(1)+(1-D)Y(0)\ Q\text{-a.s.};\ (Y(0),Y(1),U)\perp Z\mid X;\ D=1\{U\le p_{m\ell}\}\ Q\text{-a.s. on }\{Z=z_\ell,X=x_m\}\ \forall\ell,m;\ U\mid X=x_m\sim\operatorname{Uniform}[0,1]\ \forall m;\ r^Q\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\}.

lem:unconditional-margin-lp-reduction.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Fix $m$ and $s$. The factual equations at a representative arm give
\[
B^1_{msj}=\sum_{k=1}^{s}\sum_i r_{mkij},\qquad
B^0_{msi}=\sum_{k=s+1}^{K}\sum_j r_{mkij}.
\tag{1}
\]
Adjacent differences therefore identify the conditional transport margins
\[
\sum_jr_{mkij}=B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki},\qquad
\sum_ir_{mkij}=B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j}.
\tag{2}
\]
If multiple arms represent $q_s$, existence of one feasible response array forces their factual blocks to agree with the common cumulative sums in (1), so the representative is immaterial. Conversely, any nonnegative collection of cellwise couplings with margins (2) reproduces every factual equation by summing the adjacent differences. The rank-shape inequalities depend only on the row and column margins, so they are then automatic. Since $\alpha_m>0$, multiplication by $\alpha_m$ is a bijection. It changes (2) into the margins $u,v$ in the statement and gives
\[
S(r)=\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij},\qquad
\Delta(r)=\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}.
\tag{3}
\]
Deleting one redundant row-or-column equality in each transport cell does not alter its feasible arrays and makes the equality system full row rank. Maximizing the first expression in (3) subject to the second being nonpositive is exactly the program defining $V(\theta(P))$, proving the claim.
\end{proof}

snapshot.stmt: Let $P$ belong to $\mathcal P_e$ for some $e$ and choose $\ell_m(s)$ with $p_{m,\ell_m(s)}=q_s$. Define $B^d_{msh}=b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}$, $u_{mki}=\alpha_m(B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki})$, and $v_{mkj}=\alpha_m(B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j})$. The change of variables $t_{mkij}=\alpha_mr_{mkij}$ bijects $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ with the nonnegative transport arrays having row margins $u$, column margins $v$, and the fixed rank restrictions already encoded by these margins. Under $\Delta(r)\le0$, the objective and sign constraint become $\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. Consequently $\overline S_-(P)=V(\theta(P))$ for the fixed-coefficient reduced LP stated in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference.

defs.def:response-polytope: \mathcal R_\infty(P)=\{r\in\mathbb R_+^{M\times K\times J\times J}:\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}=\lambda_k\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M,\ k=1,\ldots,K;\ A_m^Pr=(b_{d\ell hm})_{d\in\{0,1\},\ell=1,\ldots,L,h=1,\ldots,J}\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J\}.

Define $B^d_{msh}=b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}$, $u_{mki}=\alpha_m(B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki})$, and $v_{mkj}=\alpha_m(B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j})$. The change of variables $t_{mkij}=\alpha_mr_{mkij}$ bijects $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ with the nonnegative transport arrays having row margins $u$, column margins $v$, and the fixed rank restrictions already encoded by these margins. Under $\Delta(r)\le0$, the objective and sign constraint become $\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. Consequently $\overline S_-(P)=V(\theta(P))$ for the fixed-coefficient reduced LP stated in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference.

snapshot.stmt:
For a finite fixed-coefficient LP $V(	heta)=\max\{s^Tt:Bt=	heta,\ g^Tt\le0,\ t\ge0\}$ with $B$ full row rank, $V$ is finite, concave, continuous and piecewise affine on the relative interior of its feasible right-hand-side domain. Its dual is $V(	heta)=\min\{y^T	heta:B^Ty+
u g\ge s,
u\ge0\}$, and $V'_	heta(h)=\min_{(y,
u)\in\mathcal D^*(	heta)}y^Th$. If the optimal active basis and reduced dual are unique, nonsingular, and strictly complementary, then $V$ is locally affine with gradient equal to the unique $y$. The analogous minimization LP with a scalar right-hand-side relaxation is convex and piecewise affine, and its derivative equals the signed unique multiplier.

For a finite fixed-coefficient LP $V(	heta)=\max\{s^Tt:Bt=	heta,\ g^Tt\le0,\ t\ge0\}$ with $B$ full row rank, $V$ is finite, concave, continuous and piecewise affine on the relative interior of its feasible right-hand-side domain. Its dual is $V(	heta)=\min\{y^T	heta:B^Ty+
u g\ge s,
u\ge0\}$, and $V'_	heta(h)=\min_{(y,
u)\in\mathcal D^*(	heta)}y^Th$. If the optimal active basis and reduced dual are unique, nonsingular, and strictly complementary, then $V$ is locally affine with gradient equal to the unique $y$. The analogous minimization LP with a scalar right-hand-side relaxation is convex and piecewise affine, and its derivative equals the signed unique multiplier.

lem:orthogonal-margin-expansion.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Because the grid is finite and strictly separated, uniform consistency of each training-fold propensity estimator makes nearest-grid classification correct in every cell with probability $1-o(1)$. Work on this event. Training empirical conditional-cell and instrument probabilities are root-$n$ consistent because their denominators have probabilities $\alpha_m\pi_{\ell m}>0$ and every training sample has order $n$.

For one coordinate suppress indices and write $b=P(A\mid Z=z_\ell,X=x_m)$ and $\pi=P(Z=z_\ell\mid X=x_m)$. The held-out augmented summand is
\[
m(W;\widetilde b,\widetilde\pi)=1\{X=x_m\}\widetilde b+
\frac{1\{X=x_m,Z=z_\ell\}}{\widetilde\pi}
(1\{A\}-\widetilde b).
\tag{1}
\]
Its expectation is $\alpha_m[\widetilde b+(\pi/\widetilde\pi)(b-\widetilde b)]$. At $(b,\pi)$ the derivatives with respect to both nuisance arguments are zero, and the exact bias is
\[
\alpha_m(\widetilde b-b)(1-\pi/\widetilde\pi),
\tag{2}
\]
a product of two $O_P(n^{-1/2})$ errors. Conditional on each training sample, the centered held-out average has the same first-order summand as (1) evaluated at truth; fixed dimension and bounded inverse propensities make replacement of estimated nuisances $o_P(n^{-1/2})$. Summing the folds with weights $n_f/n$ gives
\[
\sqrt n(\widetilde\chi-\chi)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\chi(W_r)+o_P(1).
\tag{3}
\]
The formulas for $\psi_\chi$ follow by subtracting the mean of (1), while the empirical covariate mass has score $1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m$. The calibration $C$ and adjacent-difference operator $T$ are fixed linear maps on the correct-classification event and $C\chi=\chi$, so applying $TC$ to (3) gives the asserted expansion. Its summands are bounded finite-dimensional vectors; the multivariate i.i.d. CLT yields the Gaussian limit. Strict positivity of all nonstructural recovered margins and strict rank inequalities keep the calibrated estimator in the same relative face with probability tending to one.
\end{proof}

snapshot.stmt: Under finite-support positivity, i.i.d. stable cross-fitting, consistent nearest-grid cutpoint classification, and factual-margin relative interiority, the calibrated augmented estimator $\widehat\theta=TC(\widetilde\alpha,\widetilde\eta)$ defined in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference satisfies $\sqrt n(\widehat\theta-\theta)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)\Rightarrow N(0,\Sigma_\theta)$, where $\psi_\theta=TC\psi_\chi$ and $\psi_\chi$ is given by equations (3)--(4) in that theorem's proof. The estimating equation for every $\eta$ coordinate is Neyman orthogonal with respect to its conditional-cell and instrument-propensity nuisances.

defs.def:dual-inference-handle: Handle: on each training fold, round \widehat p^{(-f)}_{m\ell} to its nearest grid point q_s, aggregate or select one arm for each represented cutpoint, and estimate \alpha_m and the arm-specific factual outcome cells. Calibrate these cells to the fixed-threshold Roy factual-law polytope (equivalently, recover the cellwise transport margins by adjacent differences) and express that polytope with a fixed full-row-rank equality system obtained by deleting redundant marginal and duplicate-arm equations. Solve the reduced primal-dual LP for \overline S_-(P), form its cross-fitted one-step estimator by composing the unique reduced dual/envelope gradient with held-out finite-cell residuals, and set \widehat\epsilon^\dagger=(\kappa_0-\widehat{\overline S}_-)_+. The rounded threshold has no first-order score on the correct-classification event. At primal or dual ties, estimate the active-face directional derivative of the reduced LP and apply a directional numerical-delta/bootstrap procedure.

assumptions.ass:iid-crossfit-sampling: W_1,\ldots,W_n are i.i.d. with law P, and the F cross-fitting folds form a partition with n_f/n\to\gamma_f\in(0,1) for every f.
assumptions.ass:factual-margin-relative-interiority: In the representative-cutpoint reduced factual parameterization, every nonstructurally-zero recovered row and column margin is strictly positive and every rank-shape inequality holds strictly.

node.statement: Under finite-support positivity, i.i.d. stable cross-fitting, consistent nearest-grid cutpoint classification, and factual-margin relative interiority, the calibrated augmented estimator $\widehat\theta=TC(\widetilde\alpha,\widetilde\eta)$ defined in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference satisfies $\sqrt n(\widehat\theta-\theta)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)\Rightarrow N(0,\Sigma_\theta)$, where $\psi_\theta=TC\psi_\chi$ and $\psi_\chi$ is given by equations (3)--(4) in that theorem's proof. The estimating equation for every $\eta$ coordinate is Neyman orthogonal with respect to its conditional-cell and instrument-propensity nuisances.

\begin{proof}
Because the grid is finite and strictly separated, uniform consistency of each training-fold propensity estimator makes nearest-grid classification correct in every cell with probability $1-o(1)$. For one coordinate suppress indices and write $b=P(A\mid Z=z_\ell,X=x_m)$ and $\pi=P(Z=z_\ell\mid X=x_m)$. The held-out augmented summand is

\frac{1\{X=x_m,Z=z_\ell\}}{\widetilde\pi}
(1\{A\}-\widetilde b).
\tag{1}

\[

Conditional on each training sample, the centered held-out average has the same first-order summand as (1) evaluated at truth; fixed dimension and bounded inverse propensities make replacement of estimated nuisances $o_P(n^{-1/2})$. Summing the folds with weights $n_f/n$ gives
\[
\sqrt n(\widetilde\chi-\chi)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\chi(W_r)+o_P(1).
\tag{3}

The formulas for $\psi_\chi$ follow by subtracting the mean of (1), while the empirical covariate mass has score $1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m$. The calibration $C$ and adjacent-difference operator $T$ are fixed linear maps on the correct-classification event and $C\chi=\chi$, so applying $TC$ to (3) gives the asserted expansion. Its summands are bounded finite-dimensional vectors; the multivariate i.i.d. CLT yields the Gaussian limit. Strict positivity of all nonstructural recovered margins and strict rank inequalities keep the calibrated estimator in the same relative face with probability tending to one.
\end{proof}

lem:k1-factual-sanity-reduction.proof_tex:
Proof. Suppose K=1. Then the resistance partition has the single cell I_1=[q_0,q_1]=[0,1], with q_0=0 and q_1=1. By ass:cutpoint-saturation, for every covariate cell m the represented treatment thresholds are exactly {q_0,q_1}={0,1}. Fix m, h, and an instrument arm ell. If p_{m ell}=1, def:response-polytope gives

[A_m^P r]_{1 ell h} = sum_{k: q_k <= p_{m ell}} sum_{i=1}^J r_{m k i h}.

Because K=1 and q_1=1=p_{m ell}, the outer index set is the singleton {1}. Therefore

[A_m^P r]_{1 ell h} = sum_{i=1}^J r_{m 1 i h}.

The third response-type index records Y(0), while the fourth records Y(1); summing over i thus leaves the Y(1)=y_h marginal.

If p_{m ell}=0, def:response-polytope instead gives

[A_m^P r]_{0 ell h} = sum_{k: q_{k-1} >= p_{m ell}} sum_{j=1}^J r_{m k h j}.

Again K=1, and q_{1-1}=q_0=0=p_{m ell}, so the outer index set is the singleton {1}. Consequently

[A_m^P r]_{0 ell h} = sum_{j=1}^J r_{m 1 h j}.

Summing over the fourth index leaves the Y(0)=y_h marginal. Thus the always-treated arm p_{m ell}=1 reveals the treated-potential-outcome marginal, whereas the never-treated arm p_{m ell}=0 reveals the untreated-potential-outcome marginal, precisely the indexing dictated by Y=DY(1)+(1-D)Y(0). QED.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:cutpoint-saturation with K=1, for every m and every instrument arm with p_{m\ell}=1, [A_m^Pr]_{1\ell h}=\sum_{i=1}^Jr_{m1ih}; for every arm with p_{m\ell}=0, [A_m^Pr]_{0\ell h}=\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m1hj}. Thus the treated block is the Y(1)=y_h marginal and the untreated block is the Y(0)=y_h marginal, exactly as required by consistency.

defs.def:response-polytope: \mathcal R_\infty(P)=\{r\in\mathbb R_+^{M\times K\times J\times J}:\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}=\lambda_k\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M,\ k=1,\ldots,K;\ A_m^Pr=(b_{d\ell hm})_{d\in\{0,1\},\ell=1,\ldots,L,h=1,\ldots,J}\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J\}.

prop:additive-cost-reduction.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Fix an observed law $P$ satisfying 	extnormal{ass:cutpoint-saturation}, and fix $r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P)$. For $m=1,\ldots,M$, $k=1,\ldots,K$, and the appropriate outcome indices, define the two cellwise marginals
\[
R_{mki}(r):=\sum_{j=1}^J r_{mkij},\qquad C_{mkj}(r):=\sum_{i=1}^J r_{mkij}.
\]
For every $m$ and every $s\in\{0,\ldots,K\}$, cutpoint saturation permits us to choose an instrument index $\ell_m(s)$ such that $p_{m,\ell_m(s)}=q_s$. Put
\[
B^1_{msj}:=b_{1,\ell_m(s),j,m},\qquad B^0_{msi}:=b_{0,\ell_m(s),i,m}.
\]
The factual equalities $A_m^P r=b$ in 	extnormal{def:response-polytope}, together with the strict ordering $q_0<\cdots<q_K$, imply
\[
B^1_{msj}=[A_m^Pr]_{1,\ell_m(s),j}
 =\sum_{v:q_v\le q_s}\sum_{i=1}^Jr_{mvij}
 =\sum_{v=1}^{s}C_{mvj}(r),
\tag{1}
\]
and
\[
B^0_{msi}=[A_m^Pr]_{0,\ell_m(s),i}
 =\sum_{v:q_{v-1}\ge q_s}\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mvij}
 =\sum_{v=s+1}^{K}R_{mvi}(r).
\tag{2}
\]
Here and below an empty sum is zero, so (1)--(2) include the boundary cutpoints $s=0$ and $s=K$. Taking adjacent differences gives, for every $k=1,\ldots,K$,
\[
C_{mkj}(r)=B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j},\qquad
R_{mki}(r)=B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki}.
\tag{3}
\]
The right-hand sides of (3) are functionals of the fixed observed law $P$ and do not depend on $r$. Consequently, every array in $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ has the same cellwise $Y(0)$ and $Y(1)$ marginals. (If more than one arm represents a cutpoint, feasibility of $r$ makes the corresponding cumulative factual margins agree, so the choice of $\ell_m(s)$ is immaterial.)

Now impose $c_{ij}=\xi_i+\zeta_j$. Using finite-sum rearrangement and (3),
\begin{align*}
\Delta(r)
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^J(\xi_i+\zeta_j)r_{mkij}\\
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^J\xi_iR_{mki}(r)+\sum_{j=1}^J\zeta_jC_{mkj}(r)\right\}\\
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k\left\{
\sum_{i=1}^J\xi_i(B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki})
+\sum_{j=1}^J\zeta_j(B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j})\right\}.
\tag{4}
\end{align*}
Expression (4) depends only on $P$ and the fixed loadings and weights, proving that $\Delta$ is constant on $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$.

Finally, if $P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon$, then $\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)$ is nonempty by 	extnormal{def:feasible-observed-law-domain}, and $\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\subseteq\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ by 	extnormal{def:association-response-polytope}. Thus the minimum and maximum in 	extnormal{def:policy-endpoints} are taken over a nonempty set on which $\Delta$ has the same value. Therefore
\[
\delta_L(P,\epsilon)=\delta_U(P,\epsilon).
\]
\end{proof}

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:cutpoint-saturation, if c_{ij}=\xi_i+\zeta_j for fixed loadings (\xi_i)_{i=1}^J and (\zeta_j)_{j=1}^J, then \Delta(r) is constant on \mathcal R_\infty(P). Hence, for every \epsilon with P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon, \delta_L(P,\epsilon)=\delta_U(P,\epsilon).

defs.def:response-polytope: \mathcal R_\infty(P)=\{r\in\mathbb R_+^{M\times K\times J\times J}:\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}=\lambda_k\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M,\ k=1,\ldots,K;\ A_m^Pr=(b_{d\ell hm})_{d\in\{0,1\},\ell=1,\ldots,L,h=1,\ldots,J}\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J\}.
defs.def:feasible-observed-law-domain: \mathcal P_\epsilon=\{P\in\mathcal P(\mathcal W):\alpha_m>0\ \forall m;\ \pi_{\ell m}>0\ \forall\ell,m;\ \{p_{m\ell}:\ell=1,\ldots,L\}=\{q_k:k=0,\ldots,K\}\ \forall m;\ \mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\ne\varnothing\}.

snapshot.stmt: For every $P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon$, the structural identified set is exactly $\operatorname{Id}^{\epsilon}_P(\delta_Q)=[\delta_L(P,\epsilon),\delta_U(P,\epsilon)]$. Fix an observed law $P$ belonging to $\mathcal P_e$ for at least one $e\ge0$. If $\mathcal R^-_\infty(P)\ne\varnothing$, then $\epsilon^\dagger(P)=(\kappa_0-\overline S_-(P))_+$. On $E_P=\{e\ge0:P\in\mathcal P_e\}$, the map $e\mapsto\delta_L(P,e)$ is finite, nonincreasing, convex, continuous relative to $E_P$, and piecewise affine. At every relative-interior point $e$ at which the association-floor dual multiplier is unique, it is differentiable and $\delta_L'(P,e)=-\mu_e$. If additionally $P\in\mathcal P_0$, $\delta_L(P,0)>0$, $\overline S_-(P)<\kappa_0$, and $\min_{r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P)}\Delta(r)<0$, then $\delta_L(P,e)>0$ for $0\le e<\epsilon^\dagger(P)$, $\delta_L(P,\epsilon^\dagger(P))\le0$, and for every $e>\epsilon^\dagger(P)$ there is $Q_e\in\mathfrak Q_e(P)$ with $\delta_{Q_e}<0$.

defs.def:association-frontier-handle: Handle: use the one-array response-type polytope \mathcal R_\epsilon(P), prove the converse by constructing a common generalized-Roy law from every feasible array, and use parametric-LP duality to compare the sign-reversing face \{r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P):\Delta(r)\le0\} with the association floor S(r)\ge\kappa_0-e.

node.statement: For every $P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon$, the structural identified set is exactly $\operatorname{Id}^{\epsilon}_P(\delta_Q)=[\delta_L(P,\epsilon),\delta_U(P,\epsilon)]$. Fix an observed law $P$ belonging to $\mathcal P_e$ for at least one $e\ge0$. If $\mathcal R^-_\infty(P)\ne\varnothing$, then $\epsilon^\dagger(P)=(\kappa_0-\overline S_-(P))_+$. On $E_P=\{e\ge0:P\in\mathcal P_e\}$, the map $e\mapsto\delta_L(P,e)$ is finite, nonincreasing, convex, continuous relative to $E_P$, and piecewise affine. At every relative-interior point $e$ at which the association-floor dual multiplier is unique, it is differentiable and $\delta_L'(P,e)=-\mu_e$. If additionally $P\in\mathcal P_0$, $\delta_L(P,0)>0$, $\overline S_-(P)<\kappa_0$, and $\min_{r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P)}\Delta(r)<0$, then $\delta_L(P,e)>0$ for $0\le e<\epsilon^\dagger(P)$, $\delta_L(P,\epsilon^\dagger(P))\le0$, and for every $e>\epsilon^\dagger(P)$ there is $Q_e\in\mathfrak Q_e(P)$ with $\delta_{Q_e}<0$.

thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Let $\ell_m(s)=\min\{\ell:p_{m\ell}=q_s\}$. The minimum cutpoint spacing is positive. Therefore ass:cutpoint-estimator-consistency implies that, simultaneously over the finite set of folds and cells, nearest-grid rounding selects every true cutpoint class and hence $\widehat\ell_m(s)=\ell_m(s)$ with probability tending to one. On this event the estimated-threshold procedure equals its oracle-classification version exactly; equality on events of probability $1-o(1)$ preserves every asserted weak limit.

Define $\eta_{dshm}=\alpha_m b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}$. After deleting redundant equations, let $C$ denote the fixed Euclidean projection onto
\[
\sum_h\eta_{1shm}=\alpha_mq_s,\qquad
\sum_h\eta_{0shm}=\alpha_m(1-q_s),
\tag{1}
\]
and let $T$ be the fixed adjacent-difference map in the statement. The population vector $\chi=(\alpha,\eta)$ obeys (1), and lem:orthogonal-margin-expansion gives
\[
\sqrt n(\widehat\theta-\theta)
=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)
\Rightarrow G\sim N(0,\Sigma_\theta),
\qquad \psi_\theta=TC\psi_\chi.
\tag{2}
\]
The raw score used here is explicit:
\[
\psi_{\alpha_m}(W)=1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m,
\tag{3}
\]
\[
\psi_{\eta_{dshm}}(W)
=b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}(1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m)
+\frac{1\{X=x_m,Z=z_{\ell_m(s)}\}}{\pi_{\ell_m(s),m}}
\bigl(1\{Y=y_h,D=d\}-b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}\bigr).
\tag{4}
\]
The augmentation is Neyman orthogonal. Indeed, if $\widetilde b,\widetilde\pi$ replace the true nuisances in the summand defining $\widetilde\eta$, its expectation is
\[
\alpha_m\left[\widetilde b+
\frac{\pi}{\widetilde\pi}(b-\widetilde b)\right].
\tag{5}
\]
Both first derivatives of (5) with respect to $\widetilde b$ and $\widetilde\pi$ vanish at $(b,\pi)$. Finite support and positive cell probabilities make the training empirical nuisances root-$n$ consistent, so the product remainder in (5) is $O_P(n^{-1})=o_P(n^{-1/2})$. This proves the orthogonal expansion rather than assuming it. Relative interiority ensures that the calibrated adjacent-difference margins remain positive and satisfy the strict rank inequalities with probability tending to one.

By lem:unconditional-margin-lp-reduction, $V(\theta)=\overline S_-(P)$. Hence the sharp frontier theorem and ass:positive-sign-radius give
\[
\epsilon^\dagger(P)=\kappa_0-V(\theta)>0.
\tag{6}
\]
Under ass:sign-lp-strong-regularity, the nonsingular active basis and strict complementarity persist in a neighborhood of $\theta$. The reduced LP value is therefore affine there, with gradient equal to its unique equality-dual optimizer $y_*$. Combining this local envelope identity with (2) and (6),
\begin{align*}
\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)
&=-\sqrt n\{V(\widehat\theta)-V(\theta)\}+o_P(1)\\
&=-y_*^\top n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)
\Rightarrow N(0,\sigma^2).
\tag{7}
\end{align*}
The positive-part map is locally the identity in (6), which is why no boundary-normal mixture appears. The variance condition makes the limit nondegenerate. Training-sample consistency, fixed dimension, overlap, and convergence of the unique dual imply that the cross-fitted estimated scores converge in $L^2(P)$ to $-y_*^\top\psi_\theta$; their empirical second moment therefore converges to $\sigma^2$. If $z_{1-\alpha/2}$ is the standard-normal quantile,
\[
\left[\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-z_{1-\alpha/2}\widehat\sigma/\sqrt n,
\widehat\epsilon^\dagger+z_{1-\alpha/2}\widehat\sigma/\sqrt n\right]\cap[0,\infty)
\tag{8}
\]
has coverage tending to $1-\alpha$; the intersection is asymptotically inactive because (6) is strict.

For the nonregular branch, on the relative interior of the sign-feasible right-hand-side domain, lem:polyhedral-lp-value-calculus gives
\[
V'_\theta(h)=\min_{(y,\nu)\in\mathcal D^*(\theta)}y^\top h,
\tag{9}
\]
where $\mathcal D^*(\theta)$ is the reduced optimal-dual face. Thus $V$ is Hadamard directionally differentiable even when (9) is nonlinear. The chain rule for the scalar positive part gives
\[
\Phi'_\theta(h)=
\begin{cases}
-V'_\theta(h),&\kappa_0>V(\theta),\\
0,&\kappa_0<V(\theta),\\
\max\{-V'_\theta(h),0\},&\kappa_0=V(\theta).
\end{cases}
\tag{10}
\]
Let $\xi_1,\ldots,\xi_n$ be i.i.d. $N(0,1)$ multipliers independent of the data and put
\[
\mathbb G_n^*=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\xi_r
(\widehat\psi_{\theta,r}-\overline{\widehat\psi}_\theta).
\tag{11}
\]
Conditional on the data, (11) is exactly Gaussian with the empirical score covariance, which converges to $\Sigma_\theta$; hence it consistently estimates $G$ in (2). By the Fang--Santos directional delta and numerical-derivative resampling theorems, $s_n\downarrow0$ and $\sqrt ns_n\to\infty$ imply
\[
\frac{\Phi(\widehat\theta+s_n\mathbb G_n^*)-\Phi(\widehat\theta)}{s_n}
\ \leadsto_P\ \Phi'_\theta(G),
\tag{12}
\]
while $\sqrt n\{\Phi(\widehat\theta)-\Phi(\theta)\}\Rightarrow\Phi'_\theta(G)$. If $c_{1-\alpha}^*$ is the conditional $(1-\alpha)$ quantile of the absolute value of (12), then $[\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-c_{1-\alpha}^*/\sqrt n,\widehat\epsilon^\dagger+c_{1-\alpha}^*/\sqrt n]\cap[0,\infty)$ has limiting coverage $1-\alpha$ whenever the distribution of $|\Phi'_\theta(G)|$ is continuous at its $(1-\alpha)$ quantile (and the usual conservative continuity-point interpretation otherwise). Relative interiority of the sign-feasible domain is used to keep every local perturbation in a finite-valued LP domain. At its boundary, $R^-_\infty$ can disappear under arbitrarily small perturbations, changing $\overline S_-$ to $-\infty$ and $\epsilon^\dagger$ to $\infty$; no finite root-$n$ directional approximation can cover that discontinuity.
\end{proof}

snapshot.stmt: Let $P$ satisfy ass:covariate-cell-positivity, ass:instrument-overlap, ass:cutpoint-saturation, ass:iid-crossfit-sampling, ass:cutpoint-estimator-consistency, ass:factual-margin-relative-interiority, and ass:positive-sign-radius. Select in each $(m,s)$ cell the least instrument arm whose cross-fitted propensity rounds to $q_s$; estimate its conditional $(Y,D)$ cells and instrument propensity on the training sample. Form the cross-fitted augmented estimators $\widetilde\eta_{dshm}=n^{-1}\sum_f\sum_{r\in I_f}[1\{X_r=x_m\}\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m}+1\{X_r=x_m,Z_r=z_{\widehat\ell_m(s)}\}(1\{Y_r=y_h,D_r=d\}-\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m})/\widehat\pi^{(-f)}_{\widehat\ell_m(s),m}]$ and $\widetilde\alpha_m=n^{-1}\sum_r1\{X_r=x_m\}$. Project $(\widetilde\alpha,\widetilde\eta)$ onto the affine constraints $\sum_h\eta_{1shm}=\alpha_mq_s$ and $\sum_h\eta_{0shm}=\alpha_m(1-q_s)$, and take adjacent differences $u_{mki}=\eta_{0,k-1,i,m}-\eta_{0,k,i,m}$ and $v_{mkj}=\eta_{1,k,j,m}-\eta_{1,k-1,j,m}$; call the resulting unconditional-margin vector $\widehat\theta$. Let $V(\theta)$ be the reduced fixed-coefficient LP $\max_{t\ge0}\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ subject to $\sum_jt_{mkij}=u_{mki}$, $\sum_it_{mkij}=v_{mkj}$ (with redundant equalities deleted), and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. Define $\widehat\epsilon^\dagger=(\kappa_0-V(\widehat\theta))_+$. Under ass:sign-lp-strong-regularity and ass:sign-radius-influence-variance, $\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)\Rightarrow N(0,\sigma^2)$ with influence function $-y_*^\top\psi_\theta(W)$, where $y_*$ is the unique reduced equality-dual optimizer and $\psi_\theta$ is the calibrated adjacent-difference finite-cell score; the plug-in score variance is consistent and the usual Wald interval has asymptotic coverage $1-\alpha$. Without primal or dual uniqueness, if the true margin vector is in the relative interior of the sign-LP feasible right-hand-side domain, the map $\Phi(\theta)=(\kappa_0-V(\theta))_+$ is Hadamard directionally differentiable. For $s_n\downarrow0$ with $\sqrt n s_n\to\infty$, the Gaussian-multiplier numerical derivative $[\Phi(\widehat\theta+s_n\mathbb G_n^*)-\Phi(\widehat\theta)]/s_n$ consistently estimates the law of $\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)$; confidence sets obtained from its absolute-value quantiles are asymptotically valid at continuity points of the limiting quantile. The relative-interior qualification is necessary to exclude the boundary at which the sign-reversal LP becomes infeasible and the finite radius can jump to infinity.

defs.def:dual-inference-handle: Handle: on each training fold, round \widehat p^{(-f)}_{m\ell} to its nearest grid point q_s, aggregate or select one arm for each represented cutpoint, and estimate \alpha_m and the arm-specific factual outcome cells. Calibrate these cells to the fixed-threshold Roy factual-law polytope (equivalently, recover the cellwise transport margins by adjacent differences) and express that polytope with a fixed full-row-rank equality system obtained by deleting redundant marginal and duplicate-arm equations. Solve the reduced primal-dual LP for \overline S_-(P), form its cross-fitted one-step estimator by composing the unique reduced dual/envelope gradient with held-out finite-cell residuals, and set \widehat\epsilon^\dagger=(\kappa_0-\widehat{\overline S}_-)_+. The rounded threshold has no first-order score on the correct-classification event. At primal or dual ties, estimate the active-face directional derivative of the reduced LP and apply a directional numerical-delta/bootstrap procedure.

assumptions.ass:iid-crossfit-sampling: W_1,\ldots,W_n are i.i.d. with law P, and the F cross-fitting folds form a partition with n_f/n\to\gamma_f\in(0,1) for every f.
assumptions.ass:factual-margin-relative-interiority: In the representative-cutpoint reduced factual parameterization, every nonstructurally-zero recovered row and column margin is strictly positive and every rank-shape inequality holds strictly.
assumptions.ass:sign-lp-strong-regularity: The full-row-rank reduced LP defining \overline S_-(P) has a unique primal optimizer, a unique reduced dual optimizer, a nonsingular active-constraint basis, and strict complementarity.
assumptions.ass:sign-radius-influence-variance: The influence function obtained by composing the reduced-LP envelope gradient with the finite-cell observed-data scores has variance \sigma^2 satisfying 0<\sigma^2<\infty.

node.statement: Let $P$ satisfy ass:covariate-cell-positivity, ass:instrument-overlap, ass:cutpoint-saturation, ass:iid-crossfit-sampling, ass:cutpoint-estimator-consistency, ass:factual-margin-relative-interiority, and ass:positive-sign-radius. Select in each $(m,s)$ cell the least instrument arm whose cross-fitted propensity rounds to $q_s$; estimate its conditional $(Y,D)$ cells and instrument propensity on the training sample. Form the cross-fitted augmented estimators $\widetilde\eta_{dshm}=n^{-1}\sum_f\sum_{r\in I_f}[1\{X_r=x_m\}\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m}+1\{X_r=x_m,Z_r=z_{\widehat\ell_m(s)}\}(1\{Y_r=y_h,D_r=d\}-\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m})/\widehat\pi^{(-f)}_{\widehat\ell_m(s),m}]$ and $\widetilde\alpha_m=n^{-1}\sum_r1\{X_r=x_m\}$. Let $V(\theta)$ be the reduced fixed-coefficient LP $\max_{t\ge0}\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ subject to $\sum_jt_{mkij}=u_{mki}$, $\sum_it_{mkij}=v_{mkj}$ (with redundant equalities deleted), and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. The relative-interior qualification is necessary to exclude the boundary at which the sign-reversal LP becomes infeasible and the finite radius can jump to infinity.

oeq:sharp-association-sign-frontier.source_fingerprint: {"kind":"openendedquestion","statement":"Can def:association-frontier-handle establish, in the displayed finite-grid generalized-Roy rank-association model, that for every \\epsilon with P\\in\\mathcal P_\\epsilon, \\operatorname{Id}^{\\epsilon}_P(\\delta_Q)=[\\delta_L(P,\\epsilon),\\delta_U(P,\\epsilon)], and, when \\mathcal R^-_\\infty(P)\\ne\\varnothing, that \\epsilon^\\dagger(P)=(\\kappa_0-\\overline S_-(P))_+ with e\\mapsto\\delta_L(P,e) nonincreasing, convex, and piecewise affine on \\{e:P\\in\\mathcal P_e\\}, having derivative -\\mu_e at relative-interior points with a unique association-floor dual multiplier \\mu_e? Under \\delta_L(P,0)>0, \\overline S_-(P)<\\kappa_0, and \\min_{r\\in\\mathcal R_\\infty(P)}\\Delta(r)<0, can it further show \\delta_L(P,e)>0 for 0\\le e<\\epsilon^\\dagger(P), \\delta_L(P,\\epsilon^\\dagger(P))\\le0, and a Q\\in\\mathfrak Q_e(P) with \\delta_Q<0 for every e>\\epsilon^\\dagger(P)?","depends_on":["lem:factual-map-and-pasting","def:policy-endpoints","def:identified-set","def:sign-reversal-polytope","def:maximal-sign-reversing-association","def:sign-flip-radius","def:association-frontier-handle"],"justification":"The question isolates the unproved common-law sharpness and robustness-boundary construction for a nonseparable policy gain-band ranking.","gap":"Tan, Blanchet, and Syrgkanis (2026) obtain a product-cost reduction, not this nonseparable common-law constrained-LP frontier. Masten and Poirier (2020, Section 2) define a breakdown frontier for binary-treatment conclusions under relaxations of random assignment and rank invariance; their observable-law functional does not cover a discrete-IV generalized-Roy response-type polytope or an association-floor sensitivity path.","consumer":"A positive resolution would let a sensitivity analysis report the smallest calibrated loss of potential-outcome rank association that permits a policy ranking to reverse."}

oeq:orthogonal-inference-for-sign-radius.source_fingerprint: {"kind":"openendedquestion","statement":"Can def:dual-inference-handle yield a cross-fitted estimator and confidence set for \\epsilon^\\dagger(P) with a root-n Gaussian limit under unique primal-dual solutions, and a Fang--Santos-valid directional procedure at dual ties, under primitive nuisance-rate and cutpoint-stability conditions?","depends_on":["oeq:sharp-association-sign-frontier","def:dual-inference-handle"],"justification":"The sign-flip radius is an optimized generated-regressor functional whose LP face may change with estimated Roy thresholds.","gap":"Ober-Reynolds (2023) studies regular OT-dual inference without generated generalized-Roy cutpoints; Fang and Santos (2019) provides the directional framework but not this orthogonal score.","consumer":"It would attach valid uncertainty statements to the association loss required to reverse a policy conclusion."}
